title: 10 1 Harder Fourier Series
body:
TOPIC 10 : FOURIER SERIES II
                   10.1 SOME HARDER PROBLEMS


Suppose that a function f has period T = 2L. Then f may be approximated by the
Fourier series                      ∞
                                   X           nπx          nπx
                      f (x) = a0 +     (an cos     + bn sin     )
                                   n=1
                                                L            L
where the Fourier coefficients a0 , an , and bn are given by

                            1
                                 RL
                      a0 = 2L         f (x) dx
                                −L


                                RL               nπx
                      an = L1        f (x) cos       dx          (n = 1, 2, . . . )
                                −L                L

                                RL               nπx
                      bn = L1        f (x) sin       dx          (n = 1, 2, . . . )
                                −L                L

Example 1 Suppose that f (x) =
                                           f (x + 2), otherwise.

                                                  n=1

Find a0 by inspection and bn using Example 2 from the last lecture. To what value does
the Fourier series converge at x = 1?

 Example 2 A periodic voltage cos(t) is passed through a half-wave rectifier which
clips the negative portion of the wave. Find the Fourier series of the resulting periodic
function.

The integrals here will cause us all sorts of problems. Let us first prove two results which
will be of use:

We close with the note that when Fourier series are developed from a complex viewpoint
we have
                                                 ∞
                                                 X
                                    f (x) =              cn einx
                                              n=−∞

with
                                            Zπ
                                        1
                                  cn =           f (x)e−inx dx.
                                       2π
                                            −π

This is then the complex form of the Fourier series for f (x). The cn are the complex
Fourier coefficients. Students are referred to the printed notes for a proof of the above
results. We tend to focus on the real case in Math2019.
